meta.title: Gastroenterology Practice Software | Endoscopy Efficiency & IBD RTM | clinIQ
meta.description: Complete guide to GI practice operations. 20% higher endoscopy throughput. IBD RTM billing. Biologic authorization tracking. Prep compliance visibility.

hero.subhead: Your endoscopy suite drives revenue — 20% more throughput is $400K/year. Your IBD patients need monitoring between infusions — that's RTM. Your prep failures waste capacity — that's preventable. clinIQ optimizes endoscopy efficiency, captures IBD RTM, and improves prep compliance.

sections.content:
GI practices generate revenue from two sources: clinic visits and procedures. Procedures — especially colonoscopy and EGD — drive the majority of revenue.

The average screening colonoscopy generates $1,500-2,500 (professional fee + facility, if physician-owned ASC). A practice doing 20 scopes/day generates $30,000-50,000/day.

**The throughput opportunity:**

Most GI practices run endoscopy suites at 70-80% efficiency. Dead time between cases, late starts, cancellations, and prep failures waste capacity.

20% improvement in throughput = 4 additional cases/day = $6,000-10,000/day = $1.5-2.5 million/year.

**The chronic disease opportunity:**

IBD patients (Crohn's, ulcerative colitis) on biologic therapy need between-visit monitoring. Symptom tracking, medication compliance, flare detection. This qualifies for RTM.

60 IBD patients × $130/month = $7,800/month = $93,600/year.

**The prep failure problem:**

Inadequate prep rates run 15-25%. Each prep failure is a wasted slot — the scope still takes time, but needs to be repeated. That's 3-5 wasted procedures/day in a busy practice.

Endoscopy suite efficiency depends on:

1. Room turnover: Time from one patient out to next patient in
2. First case on-time: Whether the 7:30 AM case starts at 7:30 AM
3. Pre-procedure readiness: Consent, labs, NPO status confirmed
4. Procedure efficiency: Actual procedure time vs allocated time
5. Recovery flow: Patients moving through recovery without bottleneck

**Where time gets wasted:**

Room ready but next patient not in pre-op. Pre-op ready but consent not signed. Consent signed but anesthesia hasn't cleared. Anesthesia cleared but room not turned over.

Without visibility, these gaps compound. By afternoon, you're running 45 minutes behind.

**What clinIQ does for endoscopy efficiency:**

Room status visibility: Each room — in procedure, turnover, ready. Recovery bays — occupied, ready for next patient.

Pre-procedure checklist: Consent, NPO, labs, driver arranged — status visible before patient comes to pre-op.

Patient pipeline: Where is each patient? Pre-op, in procedure, recovery, ready for discharge.

Turnover tracking: Case end → room ready → next patient in. Every turnover timestamped.

First case on-time: 7:30 AM case actually starts at 7:30 AM. Delays tracked, causes identified.

**The throughput math:**

10 cases/day at 80% efficiency = 8 billable procedures
10 cases/day at 100% efficiency = 10 billable procedures

2 additional procedures × $2,000 = $4,000/day = $1,000,000/year

Actual efficiency gains of 15-20% are achievable with visibility.

Inflammatory bowel disease patients — Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis — require ongoing monitoring between visits.

**What IBD patients track:**

Symptom activity:
- Stool frequency (number of BMs/day)
- Stool consistency and urgency
- Abdominal pain
- Rectal bleeding (UC) or fistula symptoms (Crohn's)
- Fatigue and general wellbeing

Medication compliance:
- Biologic injection/infusion adherence
- Oral medication compliance (mesalamine, immunomodulators)
- Steroid use (concerning if increasing)

Quality of life:
- Work/school attendance
- Social activities
- Food tolerances

**The CPT codes:**

98975: RTM setup — one-time. ~$20-25.
98977: RTM device supply. ~$50-60/month.
98980: RTM treatment management — first 20 minutes. ~$50-70/month.
98981: Additional 20-minute increments.

Typical: $120-150/patient/month.

**What clinIQ does for IBD RTM:**

IBD-specific templates: Stool frequency, urgency, bleeding, pain — the questions that matter for IBD.

Flare detection: Symptom frequency increasing? PRN steroid use? Early flare signs trigger alerts.

Disease activity scoring: Simple scoring that tracks improvement or worsening over time.

Pre-infusion assessment: For patients on infusions, pre-visit symptom check informs whether to proceed or assess for flare.

**The clinical value:**

IBD flares that progress can require hospitalization ($15,000-30,000), surgery, or permanent bowel loss. Early detection and intervention prevents these outcomes.

RTM revenue ($1,500/patient/year) plus prevented hospitalization (even one) = significant value.

IBD biologics (Humira, Remicade, Entyvio, Stelara, Skyrizi, etc.) require prior authorization.

**The authorization burden:**

Initial auth:
- Documented IBD diagnosis with objective evidence (scope, imaging, labs)
- Failure of conventional therapy (steroids, immunomodulators)
- Disease severity documentation

Ongoing auth:
- Annual or semi-annual renewals
- Response documentation
- Labs (TB, hepatitis) current

**What clinIQ does for biologic auth:**

Auth status per patient: Current biologic, auth status, expiration, doses/infusions remaining.

Expiration alerts: 'Entyvio auth expires in 21 days. Patient due for infusion next week.'

Prior medication documentation: Steroids, mesalamine, immunomodulators — trials documented with dates and outcomes.

Lab tracking: TB, hepatitis, CBC, CMP — required labs tracked with dates.

**Switching biologics:**

Patients who fail one biologic need documentation to switch to another:
- Primary non-response vs secondary loss of response
- Prior biologic duration and reason for discontinuation
- Washout period (if required by new drug)

clinIQ tracks this history to support authorization for next-line agents.

Inadequate bowel prep is the single biggest waste of endoscopy capacity. National inadequate prep rates run 15-25%. Each inadequate prep means:

- The procedure slot is partially wasted (scope happens but needs repeat)
- Patient undergoes another prep
- Another slot is used for repeat procedure
- Adenoma detection rate suffers

**Why prep fails:**

Patient didn't understand instructions. Patient didn't finish the prep. Patient ate the day before. Patient took incomplete prep (didn't do second dose). Patient has baseline constipation that wasn't addressed.

**What clinIQ does for prep compliance:**

Pre-procedure reminders:
- Day -7: Diet modification reminder
- Day -3: Clear liquids reminder, prep pickup reminder
- Day -1 AM: First prep dose reminder
- Day -1 PM: Second prep dose reminder
- Day 0: NPO reminder, arrival time

Interactive confirmation: Patients confirm they've completed each step. No confirmation? Staff outreach.

High-risk identification: Patients with history of inadequate prep flagged for extended prep or intervention.

**The math:**

20 scopes/day × 20% inadequate prep = 4 wasted procedures/day

Reducing to 10% = 2 fewer wasted procedures/day = ~$4,000/day saved = $1,000,000/year

Prep compliance intervention is one of the highest-ROI improvements in GI.

GI clinic visits vary:

- New patient consultation: 30-45 minutes
- Follow-up: 15-20 minutes
- IBD check: 20-30 minutes
- Pre-procedure: 15-20 minutes
- Post-procedure: 10-15 minutes
- Results visit: 15-20 minutes

The schedule template often doesn't match reality.

**What clinIQ does for clinic flow:**

Visit type awareness: Different visit types have different expected durations.

Queue visibility: Who's waiting? Who's with MA? Who's ready for provider?

Procedure results integration: When patient comes for results visit, path/scope findings visible.

IBD patient flagging: IBD patients may need more time. Pre-visit symptom data (from RTM) informs whether routine follow-up or flare management.

**The mixed workflow:**

GI providers often split time between clinic and procedures. Schedule visibility for both is essential.

clinIQ shows: 'Morning: Clinic until 11am. Procedures 11am-3pm. Clinic 3-5pm.' Provider knows what's coming.

GI implementation covers endoscopy efficiency, IBD RTM, and prep compliance.

**Week 1: Configuration**

We map your workflows: procedure suite, clinic, recovery, authorization.

Endoscopy tracking configured: Room status, patient pipeline, turnover metrics.

RTM templates configured: IBD, chronic GI conditions.

Prep reminders configured: Multi-day reminder sequence.

**Week 2: Training**

Endoscopy staff learns room and patient tracking. Clinic staff learns flow. Coordinators learn auth tracking. All staff learn prep reminder system.

**Week 3: Go-Live**

Endoscopy visibility active. Prep reminders running. RTM enrollment starting. Clinic flow live.

**ROI Timeline:**

Month 1: Prep compliance improving immediately. Endoscopy visibility active.

Month 3: Measurable throughput improvement. RTM billing at scale. Prep failure rate down.

Month 6: Full optimization. 15-20% throughput improvement. $6,000-8,000/month RTM. Prep compliance at new baseline.

**ROI Math:**

Throughput improvement: 2 additional procedures/day × $2,000 × 250 days = $1,000,000/year (capacity)

Prep compliance: 10% reduction in failures × 4 recovered procedures/day × $500/wasted slot × 250 days = $500,000/year

RTM revenue: 60 patients × $130/month = $7,800/month = $93,600/year

Total: $1.5 million+ annual impact.

**Investment:**

Professional: $499/month

Implementation: $750 one-time

faqs.q: How do you improve endoscopy throughput?
faqs.a: Room status visibility, patient pipeline tracking, turnover measurement, first-case on-time improvement. You see where time is wasted and can address specific bottlenecks.

faqs.q: Which GI conditions qualify for RTM?
faqs.a: IBD (Crohn's, UC) is the primary RTM opportunity. Chronic conditions like GERD, IBS, and chronic liver disease may also qualify depending on monitoring requirements.
Multi-day text/email sequence: diet modification, prep pickup, first dose, second dose, NPO, arrival. Patients confirm completion. No confirmation triggers outreach.
Yes. Auth status per patient per drug. Expiration alerts. Prior biologic trial documentation. Lab tracking for required screenings.

faqs.q: What about ASC vs hospital-based endoscopy?
faqs.a: Works for both. ASC integration may be simpler (you control the environment). Hospital-based requires coordination with facility systems.

faqs.q: How do you identify high-risk prep patients?
faqs.a: History of inadequate prep, baseline constipation, certain medications, prior incomplete procedures. These patients flagged for extended prep or intervention.

faqs.q: Does this work with procedure scheduling systems?
faqs.a: We integrate with major scheduling systems. Specific integration scope depends on your systems.

faqs.q: How fast can we see ROI?
faqs.a: Prep compliance improvement and endoscopy visibility are immediate. RTM revenue builds over 2-3 months as patients enroll.

testimonial.quote: We were wasting 4-5 procedures a day to bad preps. The reminder system cut that in half. And the IBD RTM is paying for itself many times over.
